planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn or divide in the green after flowering.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in cool, leafy soil in light shade. One of the refined Greatorex double snowdrops, it carries a neat, well-filled rosette of green-marked inner segments. It flowers in late winter and is a good choice for gardeners wanting a tidy double.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich soil in a partly shaded border.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in leaf-rich, free-draining soil. This early Greatorex double opens a full, rounded rosette of green-tipped segments, often earlier than many doubles. Enjoy the blooms from mid winter and divide congested clumps in the green.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile, moisture-retentive soil in light shade.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil that does not dry out. This sought-after yellow snowdrop replaces the usual green ovary and segment marks with clear yellow, giving a golden glow. It flowers in late winter and settles best in a consistently cool, moist position.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich, free-draining soil in a sheltered, partly shaded spot.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in leafy, well-drained soil. This admired early cultivar bears large, well-shaped flowers on long pedicels, opening among the first of the season. It can be slower to increase than some, so give it a settled, undisturbed home.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile soil in light shade.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil. This robust old cultivar hangs large flowers on long pedicels that catch the wind, and it increases freely into good clumps. It flowers in late winter and makes a dependable garden performer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich soil in a partly shaded border.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in leafy, free-draining soil. This classic cultivar is prized for inner segments almost entirely washed with deep green, so the flower looks solidly marked when it opens. It blooms in late winter and forms neat, well-behaved clumps.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil. This sturdy cultivar has strongly green-marked inner segments and a robust constitution that makes it easy in the garden. It flowers in late winter and bulks up dependably.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile, free-draining soil in a partly shaded spot.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil. One of the oldest garden snowdrops, this reliable cultivar produces two flowers per bulb in a good year and blooms a little later than most, extending the season. It is long-lived and increases steadily.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in gritty, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: Set bulbs three to four inches deep in free-draining soil enriched with leaf mould. This vigorous giant-snowdrop selection carries large, well-rounded flowers on tall stems and makes a strong garden clump. It flowers in late winter and is one of the more robust named elwesii forms.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich, well-drained soil in a partly shaded position.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in cool, leafy soil. This popular cultivar shows bold green markings on both the inner and the outer segments, giving strongly patterned flowers. It blooms in late winter and forms tidy clumps.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile, free-draining soil in light shade.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil. This much-admired cultivar is instantly recognizable for its rounded, seersucker-textured outer segments that flare into a puckered, balloon-like flower. It blooms in late winter and increases slowly, so give it an undisturbed spot.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich soil in a partly shaded border.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in leaf-rich, free-draining soil. This quirky double faces its flowers outward rather than nodding, showing a ruffled cluster of green-and-white segments that some liken to a small rosette. It flowers in late winter and is a favorite among collectors for its unusual poise.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn or divide clumps in the green after flowering.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil in light shade. This charming double opens a neat rosette of green-tipped inner segments and increases well for a double form. Enjoy the blooms in late winter and lift crowded clumps in the green to keep them thriving.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in cool, moisture-retentive, humus-rich soil in light shade.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in leaf-rich soil that stays cool and never bakes. This desirable yellow snowdrop shows a yellow ovary and yellow segment markings in place of the usual green. It flowers in late winter and does best given a settled, consistently moist position.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile, free-draining soil in a partly shaded spot.

directions: Set bulbs three to four inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil. This distinctive pleated-snowdrop cultivar has broad, rounded, seersucker-textured outer segments and vigorous broad leaves. It flowers in late winter and forms a handsome, substantial clump.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in cool, leafy soil. This unusual cultivar carries six outer and six inner segments, giving a full, many-petalled flower with a distinctive symmetry. It blooms in late winter and rewards close inspection.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in cool, humus-rich soil in light shade.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in leaf-rich, moisture-retentive soil. This rare yellow-marked selection combines the glossy green leaves of the species with a golden ovary and yellow inner markings. It flowers in late winter and is best given a cool, settled position to establish slowly.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn or divide in the green after flowering.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in humus-rich, free-draining soil in light shade. Another of the Greatorex doubles, it opens a full rosette of well-marked green inner segments held in a rounded flower. It blooms in late winter and makes a good, reliable double for the garden.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich soil in a partly shaded border.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in cool, leaf-rich, well-drained soil. This Greatorex double bears a neat, symmetrical rosette of green-tipped segments on a sturdy stem. Enjoy the blooms in late winter and divide congested clumps in the green.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in fertile, well-drained soil in light shade.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in humus-rich soil in a partly shaded spot. This popular cultivar carries large, rounded flowers on relatively long pedicels, giving a generous look despite its modest height. It flowers in late winter and increases into good clumps.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in humus-rich, free-draining soil in light shade.

directions: Set bulbs three inches deep in cool, leaf-rich soil. This cultivar is distinctive for its glossy, almost poculiform flowers where the inner segments are nearly as long as the outer, and for its bright green leaves. It flowers in late winter and rewards a settled position.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in drifts for the strongest early color.

directions: Plant the bulbs three inches deep in fertile, well-drained ground and water them in once. This selection carries larger, more intensely deep-blue flowers than the plain species and multiplies readily. Expect vivid blooms in early spring under deciduous trees before the canopy leafs out.

planting.note: Plant in autumn where the pure-white bells can brighten shady corners.

directions: Tuck the bulbs three inches deep into humus-rich soil, spacing them a few inches apart for a natural look. The clear-white nodding bells glow in dappled shade and pair well with blue forms. Foliage should be left standing until it withers naturally so the colony strengthens each year.

planting.note: Plant in autumn; this is one of the earliest bulbs to flower.

directions: Plant bulbs about three inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained spot in fall. The pale ice-blue stars, each veined with a deeper stripe, open almost at ground level then rise as the stems stretch. Look for the very first blooms in late winter to early spring, often before the crocuses.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in groups for a woodland effect.

directions: Set the small bulbs about three inches deep in leafy, well-drained soil beneath shrubs or trees. Each stem bears a loose spray of star-shaped violet-blue flowers above a pair of narrow leaves. It naturalizes freely from seed, so leave clumps undisturbed to spread and bloom in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn where the soft-pink stars will not be overpowered by bolder blooms.

directions: Plant the bulbs three inches deep in cool, humus-rich soil that drains freely. This form trades the usual blue for delicate rose-pink stars carried on short stems. Combine it with white and blue squills for a gentle spring tapestry and let the foliage die back on its own.

planting.note: Plant in fall in frost-free areas; lift or mulch where winters are severe.

directions: Plant the bulbs shallowly in sharply drained soil in full sun, leaving the tops just at the surface. This form produces broad conical trusses of pure-white starry flowers over glossy strap leaves. Water during growth, then let the bulbs bake and rest through summer for the best late-spring display.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in an open, sunny position.

directions: Plant the bulbs about three inches deep in ordinary well-drained soil that stays reasonably moist in spring. Slender stems carry airy spikes of many small amethyst-blue stars later than most squills. It flowers in late spring into early summer and naturalizes gently in a sunny border or rock garden.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a spot that can be left undisturbed to naturalize.

directions: Set the bulbs three inches deep in humus-rich, well-drained soil, ideally beneath deciduous shrubs. Loose clusters of bright starry blue flowers open on short stems in mid spring. Allow the leaves to ripen fully before removing them so the clumps thicken over the years.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs in autumn in a lean, gritty, sunny site.

directions: Plant the tiny bulbs two to three inches deep in poor, very well-drained soil that dries out in summer. This coastal native forms tight rosettes topped by short heads of pale violet-blue stars. It suits rock gardens and troughs, blooming in late spring and resenting rich, damp conditions.

planting.note: Plant the large bulbs in autumn in a warm, sunny border.

directions: Set the sizable bulbs about four inches deep in fertile, free-draining soil where they can spread by offsets. Tall stems rise well above the foliage carrying long tapering racemes of many soft lilac-blue flowers. This is a bold, statuesque squill for late spring; give it room and a sheltered, sunny position.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a warm, gritty, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the bulbs about three inches deep in sharply drained soil in a sheltered, sunny corner. Each bulb sends up short spikes packed with many small pale blue stars in early to mid spring. It appreciates a summer baking once the leaves die down and slowly forms tidy clumps.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall where the soft pink stars can be seen up close.

directions: Set the bulbs about three inches deep in loose, well-drained soil in sun or light shade. This selection bears larger flowers in a warm lilac-pink with pale eyes on sturdy stems. It naturalizes willingly and gives a fresh alternative to the usual blue in early spring plantings.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in bold drifts for maximum impact.

directions: Plant the bulbs three inches deep in well-drained ground and let them settle in undisturbed. This vigorous form carries a heavier crop of large sky-blue, white-eyed stars than the species. Its early flowers are excellent for naturalizing in lawns and under deciduous trees.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a rock garden or at the front of a border.

directions: Plant the bulbs two to three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in a bright position. This species carries a few relatively large upward-facing blue stars with broad white centers per stem. It is ideal for rock gardens and troughs and flowers dependably in early spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn where the clean white flowers will brighten a shady bed.

directions: Set the bulbs about three inches deep in well-drained, humus-rich soil in light shade. This form opens pure-white stars that light up the ground before most perennials wake. Interplant it with blue chionodoxa and squills for a crisp, freshening effect in early spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in drifts for the deepest early blue.

directions: Plant the small bulbs three inches deep in freely draining soil in sun or dappled shade. This species is prized for its rich gentian-blue stars with only a tiny white eye. It self-seeds readily to knit together colonies and blooms in very early spring.

planting.note: Plant this intergeneric hybrid's bulbs in autumn in a woodland setting.

directions: Plant the bulbs about three inches deep in cool, humus-rich, well-drained soil. This natural hybrid between alpine squill and glory-of-the-snow bears loose sprays of deep violet-blue stars that blend the traits of both parents. It thrives in light shade and flowers reliably in early spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in a warm, sunny, well-drained position.

directions: Set the bulbs three to four inches deep in lean, gritty soil that bakes dry in summer. Slim stems carry open conical spikes of starry blue flowers later in the season than most squills. This Iberian species suits a hot, sunny bank and blooms in late spring into early summer.

planting.note: Grow in a frost-free border or a container that can be moved under cover.

directions: Plant the large bulb with its shoulders at the soil surface in a rich, very free-draining mix in full sun. Sturdy stems carry dense spires of many small mauve-blue flowers above broad glossy leaves. Protect it from frost, keep it drier during dormancy, and enjoy the blooms in spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in a warm, well-drained pocket of the garden.

directions: Tuck bulbs three to four inches deep in sharply drained soil, grouping them for a fuller show. Keep the site dry once the leaves yellow in early summer. Rich royal-blue blooms marked with a yellow blaze open very early, ahead of most other spring bulbs.

planting.note: Set bulbs out in fall before hard frost arrives.

directions: Plant three to four inches deep in lean, well-drained ground at the front of a border or in a rock garden. Allow the grassy leaves to ripen fully before they fade. Deep reddish-purple flowers with a gold ridge appear in late winter and carry a soft violet scent.

planting.note: Plant the bulbs in autumn in full sun.

directions: Bury bulbs about three inches deep in gritty soil that drains freely. A hot, dry summer rest helps them return each year. Soft pale-blue petals with a gold and white central patch open in earliest spring on short stems.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set the bulbs three to four inches deep and cluster them where you can see them up close. Let the foliage die back naturally and keep the ground on the dry side afterward. Dark violet-purple flowers with a bold white-flecked blaze open in late winter.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn ahead of the freeze.

directions: Plant three inches deep in a bright, freely draining bed or trough. Good drainage and a dry summer are the keys to lasting clumps. Sky-blue flowers with a yellow-and-white throat brighten the garden in late winter and early spring.

planting.note: Plant this dwarf hybrid iris in autumn in sharp-draining soil.

directions: Set bulbs three to four inches deep in gritty soil in a warm, sunny spot. This vigorous hybrid multiplies well when kept dry in summer. Its unusual pale ice-blue flowers are intricately netted in deeper blue and yellow, opening in very early spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sheltered, sunny position.

directions: Plant bulbs three to four inches deep in well-drained soil where they will be dry in summer. The sturdy flowers often open before the leaves have fully emerged. Rich reddish-purple blooms with a gold-marked fall appear in late winter and stand up well to cold weather.

planting.note: Plant the bulbs in autumn before the ground hardens.

directions: Set bulbs about three inches deep in a bright, sharply drained pocket. Keep the site dry once foliage fades to encourage a good return. Pale blue flowers heavily spotted and veined in deep blue, with a white and yellow blaze, open in earliest spring.

planting.note: Plant this yellow dwarf iris deeply in autumn.

directions: Plant bulbs a generous four to five inches deep, which helps them stay as single flowering bulbs rather than splitting into non-blooming offsets. Give them full sun and lean, fast-draining soil. Bright lemon-yellow flowers dotted with green appear in late winter on very short stems.

planting.note: Plant in autumn, handling the fleshy roots gently.

directions: Plant the bulbs about four inches deep, taking care not to snap the thick storage roots beneath. Choose a hot, well-drained spot that dries out in summer. Glossy corn-like leaves carry several white and golden-yellow flowers in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in fall in a sunny, summer-dry site.

directions: Set the bulbs four to five inches deep, protecting the brittle roots as you plant. A raised bed or slope that bakes in summer suits this tall Juno best. Pale lilac to nearly white flowers with a yellow-blotched crest line the leafy stems in mid-spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in mild zones, or spring where winters are severe.

directions: Plant bulbs four to five inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun. In colder gardens add a winter mulch or plant in early spring for summer bloom. Elegant orchid-like flowers on tall slender stems open in late spring and make excellent long-lasting cut flowers.

planting.note: Plant in fall in mild climates for late-spring bloom.

directions: Set bulbs four to five inches deep in groups for the strongest display. Water while in growth, then let the soil dry as the leaves yellow. Deep violet-blue flowers with a slim gold signal open in late spring and last well when cut.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in well-drained, sunny beds.

directions: Plant about four inches deep and keep evenly moist during active growth. Full sun and sharp drainage give the tallest, straightest stems. Crisp white flowers marked with a small yellow blaze appear in late spring and are prized for cutting.

planting.note: Plant in fall for bloom the following late spring.

directions: Bury bulbs four to five inches deep in loose, fertile soil in a bright bed. Feed lightly in spring and reduce watering as the foliage fades. Soft sky-blue and pale-lavender flowers with a yellow blaze open on tall stems in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in mild-winter gardens.

directions: Plant bulbs four to five inches deep and space them a few inches apart in drifts. Keep the bed sunny and free-draining and let the bulbs dry out after flowering. Bicolored blooms pair sky-blue standards with deeper purple-blue falls and a yellow signal in late spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in a warm, sunny border.

directions: Set bulbs about four inches deep in fertile soil that never stays soggy. Water during growth and ease off once the leaves begin to yellow. Striking white standards over golden-yellow falls open in late spring on strong cutting stems.

planting.note: Plant in autumn where winters are mild.

directions: Plant four to five inches deep in groups for the best effect. Give full sun and good drainage, and keep the soil drier after bloom. Warm golden-yellow flowers open in late spring and hold their color well in the vase.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall in a sunny, sharply drained bed.

directions: Plant bulbs four inches deep and cluster them for a soft, luminous grouping. Water while growing and allow the foliage to ripen before it fades. Pale silvery-blue flowers touched with white and a small yellow blaze appear in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn for late-spring flowers.

directions: Set bulbs four to five inches deep in fertile, freely draining soil in full sun. Keep them watered through spring growth, then dry through dormancy. Crisp white standards above bright yellow falls make a clean bicolor display in late spring.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in warm, well-drained ground.

directions: Plant bulbs about four inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained bed that dries in summer. This wild parent of the Dutch iris blooms a little later than its hybrids. Slender stems carry blue, violet, or yellow flowers with a golden blaze in late spring and early summer.

planting.note: Plant in autumn in cooler, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant bulbs four to five inches deep in rich soil that holds some moisture through the growing season. Unlike its drought-loving cousins, this iris prefers cooler, damper conditions and dislikes baking. Large blue-violet flowers with a yellow ridge open in early summer, the latest of the bulbous irises.

planting.note: Plant corms in autumn before the ground freezes.

directions: Plant corms three to four inches deep in well-drained soil, pointed side up. They naturalize readily in lawns and borders and return larger each year. Pure white goblet-shaped flowers open in early spring, welcome after a long winter.

planting.note: Plant corms in fall in a sunny or lightly shaded spot.

directions: Set corms three to four inches deep and cluster them for the boldest patches. They tolerate a range of soils as long as they drain well. Silvery-white flowers boldly striped in deep purple open in early spring and close on cloudy days.

planting.note: Plant these early snow crocus corms in autumn.

directions: Plant corms two to three inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil in full sun. These smaller snow crocus bloom even earlier than the Dutch types and multiply freely. Soft creamy-yellow flowers with golden throats open in late winter and attract early bees.

planting.note: Plant tubers after the last frost once soil has warmed.

directions: Plant tubers about four to six inches deep with the growth eye facing up after frost has passed. Pinch the growing tips early to encourage bushy, well-branched plants and stake as they grow. Semi-double scarlet flowers glow against dark bronze foliage from midsummer to frost; lift and store tubers over winter in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant soaked tubers in early spring, or in fall in mild-winter areas.

directions: Soak the knobby tubers for a few hours, then plant them two inches deep in cool, moist, well-drained soil. They flower best in the cool weather of spring or, in mild climates, from a fall planting. Vivid poppy-like blooms in red, blue, and white with dark centers appear over several weeks and are lovely for cutting.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring, or fall in frost-free gardens.

directions: Plant corms two inches deep with the pointed tip up in well-drained soil and a spot with cool nights. They need support as the arching stems fill with buds. Intensely fragrant funnel-shaped flowers open along one side of each stem; lift the corms after bloom where frost occurs.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in soil that stays moist in spring.

directions: Plant bulbs four to five inches deep in fertile soil that holds spring moisture but is not waterlogged in summer. Unlike most bulbs it tolerates heavier, damper ground and naturalizes in meadows. Tall spires of star-shaped blue or creamy-white flowers open in late spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in autumn in full sun and good drainage.

directions: Plant bulbs about three times their depth, roughly six inches, in well-drained soil in full sun. The strappy foliage often yellows as the flowers open, so site them behind other plants. Rounded heads of deep purple starry florets rise on tall stems in late spring and dry to attractive seed heads.

planting.note: Plant corms in spring, or fall in frost-free gardens.

directions: Plant corms three inches deep in a sunny, sharply drained spot and water while in growth. In colder zones grow them in pots or lift the corms after the leaves fade. Wiry stems carry starry cup-shaped flowers in vivid colors, often with a dark eye, in early summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late spring with necks at the soil surface in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set the bulbs shallowly with their long stalks in mind, choosing a warm base-of-wall position for the best display. This vigorous selection carries darker rose-pink blooms on taller stems than the ordinary species. Expect the umbels of wavy-edged flowers in autumn once the strappy leaves have died back.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late spring, leaving the necks proud of the soil.

directions: Plant with the bulb shoulders at the surface in sharply drained soil that bakes in summer. This cultivar bears glistening silvery-pink flowers with a faint sheen on strong stems. Cut a few stems for the vase as they open in autumn, where they last well.

planting.note: Plant shallowly in late spring in a hot, sheltered position.

directions: Nestle bulbs into free-draining soil with the neck exposed and top-dress with grit for extra drainage. This selection offers deep, saturated pink flowers held in generous heads. Watch for the leafless flower stems to emerge in early autumn as the season cools.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late spring with the necks above soil level.

directions: Site the bulbs shallowly at the foot of a sunny wall where summer heat ripens them. The pale form opens near-white flowers often flushed with the faintest pink at the petal bases. Allow clumps to congest over several seasons for the fullest autumn show.

planting.note: Pot up dormant bulbs in late summer, keeping the top half exposed.

directions: Grow this frost-tender species in pots of gritty compost with the bulb neck standing well above the surface. Keep the pots dry through the summer dormancy, then resume watering as the flower stems appear. Iridescent, glittering flowers in scarlet, orange, or pink unfurl in autumn before the leaves grow.

planting.note: Pot dormant bulbs in late summer under glass, neck exposed.

directions: Plant in a snug clay pot of free-draining mix and give the bulb a warm, dry summer rest. This large-flowered form produces bold heads of glistening orange-scarlet blooms. Move plants under cover before frost, as they will not survive freezing.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late spring with necks at or just above the soil.

directions: Set the small bulbs shallowly in gritty soil in a warm, protected corner or a cold greenhouse in colder areas. The dainty flowers have narrow, strongly crinkled petals in soft pink. Blooms appear late in autumn and into early winter, later than most nerines.

planting.note: Plant the small bulbs shallowly in late spring in sharp-draining soil.

directions: Grow this compact species in a raised bed or pot where its thread-fine, grassy leaves can be appreciated. Unlike many nerines it often keeps some foliage year-round, so avoid a complete dry-out. Loose heads of small pink flowers appear in autumn on slender stems.

planting.note: Plant small bulbs shallowly in spring in gritty, well-drained soil.

directions: This miniature nerine suits a trough or alpine pot filled with a free-draining, gritty mix. Its narrow leaves persist through much of the year, so keep it lightly moist rather than baking it fully dry. Small, delicate pink flowers with narrow petals open in autumn.

planting.note: Pot dormant bulbs in late summer with the neck above the surface.

directions: Give this winter-growing species a warm, dry summer rest and gritty compost in a pot or sheltered bed. Resume watering as the flower stems push up in autumn, followed by the leaves. The pink flowers have narrow, wavy-margined petals and appear on short stems.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in moisture-retentive but well-drained soil.

directions: Unlike its drought-loving relatives, this summer-growing marsh species prefers soil that stays damp during the growing season. Plant the bulbs shallowly and keep them watered while in active growth. Tall stems carry heads of pink flowers in late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer, necks near the surface.

directions: Plant these fertile, seed-setting bulbs shallowly in humus-rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade. This slightly smaller variety produces the same striking red, recurved flowers in early autumn. Allow the clump to naturalize undisturbed, as spider lilies resent frequent moving.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer with tops about an inch below the surface.

directions: The hardiest of the group, plant bulbs a little deeper in a spot where the spring foliage has room to flop. Strap-like leaves emerge in spring, die back by early summer, and then bare stems of fragrant, trumpet-shaped pink flowers surprise you in late summer. Leave clumps in place for years to build a strong display.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer with tops just below the surface.

directions: One of the hardier species, plant bulbs an inch deep in humus-rich soil in light shade. The pink, trumpet-shaped flowers are distinctively brushed with iridescent blue at the petal tips. Spring leaves die back before the late-summer bloom, so mark the spot to avoid disturbing dormant bulbs.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer with tops just below the surface.

directions: A hardy, spring-leafing species, plant bulbs a little below the surface in fertile, well-drained soil. Bright yellow flowers with wavy, reflexed petals open on bare stems in mid to late summer. The strappy foliage grows in spring and dies away before flowering, so combine it with later perennials.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer with tops just below the surface.

directions: Plant this hardy Japanese species shallowly in cool, humus-rich soil that stays lightly moist. Its upright, orange-red flowers are less spidery than the red spider lily, with shorter stamens. Spring foliage precedes the late-summer bloom, so allow it to ripen fully before it dies back.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer, tops just below the soil surface.

directions: Give this species rich, well-drained soil in dappled shade and plant the bulbs an inch deep. The flowers open pale and deepen to rose-pink, each petal marked with a darker central stripe. Bloom comes in late summer on bare stems, with the leaves following in autumn.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer with tops just below the surface.

directions: This tall, hardy species wants deep, fertile soil and a partly shaded position for its large flowers. Long-tubed white trumpets, sometimes flushed with pink or yellow, open on stout bare stems in late summer. Spring leaves die back well before bloom, so interplant with shade perennials to fill the gap.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer with necks at the surface.

directions: Plant this evergreen-leafed species shallowly in a mild-winter garden or a container in colder zones. Curled, spidery white flowers with wavy petals appear on bare stems in early autumn. Because the foliage grows through winter, protect it from hard frost and keep the soil merely moist.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer, tops just below the surface.

directions: A hardy, spring-leafing species, plant bulbs in fertile soil in light shade and leave them settled. Soft primrose-yellow flowers that fade toward cream open on bare stems in late summer. The strap leaves emerge in spring and vanish by summer, so pair with foliage that lasts.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer with tops just below the surface.

directions: This hardy hybrid combines pink coloring with a blue sheen and grows readily in well-drained, humus-rich soil. Plant the bulbs an inch deep in part shade and leave the clumps to settle for best flowering. Rose-pink flowers tipped with iridescent blue open on bare stems in late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in late summer with necks near the surface.

directions: Plant this natural hybrid shallowly in fertile, free-draining soil in dappled light. Its spidery, recurved flowers open pale straw-yellow, sometimes speckled or flushed pink with age. Blooms rise on leafless stems in early autumn, with the foliage following after.

planting.note: Pot the bulb indoors for winter bloom or set outdoors after frost in mild zones.

directions: Nest the bulb in a snug pot with the upper third sitting above the soil line and water sparingly until the flower stalk appears. Keep it in bright warmth and give a quarter turn every few days so the stem grows straight. Expect large soft-pink and white blooms roughly six to ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: Force indoors from late autumn for holiday color or grow on a bright sill.

directions: Plant the bulb shoulders-up in a heavy pot that will not tip under the tall stem. Water lightly at first, then increase once green growth is underway and feed a balanced fertilizer every couple of weeks. Deep velvety-red trumpets open in about six to nine weeks.

planting.note: Best forced indoors; move outside only after all frost has passed in warm zones.

directions: Set the bulb in fresh potting mix with its neck exposed and place it somewhere warm and bright. Keep the soil barely moist until the bud emerges, then water more freely and turn the pot for even stems. Bold red flowers marked with a white central star appear within six to ten weeks.

planting.note: Pot up in autumn or winter indoors for clean white blooms.

directions: Plant so the widest part of the bulb sits at soil level and roots have room below. Water modestly and keep in strong light, feeding lightly once leaves develop. Crisp white petals rimmed in a thin red line open about six to ten weeks after potting.

planting.note: Start indoors in November for pure white flowers by the holidays.

directions: Firm the bulb into a pot only slightly wider than itself, leaving the neck clear of the mix. Water sparingly until the scape rises, then keep evenly moist in warm, bright conditions. Snowy-white trumpets emerge in roughly six to nine weeks.

planting.note: Double-flowered type best grown indoors in a bright, warm room.

directions: Pot with the bulb shoulders above the surface and stake early, since the heavy double heads can pull the stem over. Water carefully and feed lightly through active growth. Large ruffled white flowers blushed with soft pink appear in about seven to eleven weeks.

planting.note: Force indoors for boldly striped winter flowers.

directions: Set the bulb high in the pot and water lightly until growth begins. Give it the brightest window you have and rotate the pot for a straight scape. Red-and-white striped blooms open around six to ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: Grow indoors for vivid red bloom stalks.

directions: Anchor the bulb in a weighty container with its top third exposed. Water modestly at first, then more generously as the flower stem lengthens, feeding every couple of weeks. Bright scarlet trumpets appear in roughly six to nine weeks.

planting.note: Pot indoors for soft apricot-salmon blooms.

directions: Plant the bulb with shoulders clear of the soil and keep barely moist until the bud shows. Grow in warm, bright light and turn the pot regularly. Warm salmon-apricot flowers open about six to ten weeks after potting.

planting.note: Large double type; grow indoors with support for the tall stems.

directions: Set the bulb high in a stable pot and add a stake early to hold the weighty flower heads. Water carefully until growth starts, then keep evenly moist and feed lightly. Full double blooms in peachy-pink with darker veining open in about seven to eleven weeks.

planting.note: Force indoors for striped, star-patterned flowers.

directions: Pot the bulb with its neck exposed and water lightly until the scape emerges. Keep in bright, steady warmth and rotate for straight stems. White petals brushed with red stripes open roughly six to ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: Grow indoors for delicately striped pink-and-white blooms.

directions: Firm the bulb into fresh mix, keeping the top third proud of the surface. Water sparingly until the bud appears, then increase and feed lightly. Slender white flowers penciled with rosy stripes open in about six to ten weeks.

planting.note: Double-flowered type; support the heavy stems indoors.

directions: Plant high in the pot and stake early to carry the full double heads. Water carefully until active growth, then keep evenly moist and feed every couple of weeks. Layered white-and-red blooms appear in about seven to eleven weeks.

planting.note: Bold double red; grow indoors with stem support.

directions: Set the bulb with shoulders above the soil in a heavy pot and add a support ring. Water lightly at first, then more freely as buds swell, and feed on a regular schedule. Densely petaled deep-red blooms open in about seven to eleven weeks.

planting.note: Force indoors for very dark red flowers.

directions: Pot the bulb high and water modestly until the stalk rises. Keep in bright warmth and turn for even growth. Rich, near-burgundy red trumpets open roughly six to ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: Grow indoors for large ruffled red-and-white flowers.

directions: Nest the bulb with its neck clear of the mix and keep barely damp until buds form. Provide strong light and steady warmth, feeding lightly during growth. Large frilled red petals edged and streaked with white appear in about six to ten weeks.

planting.note: Each bloom is uniquely brushed; grow indoors in bright light.

directions: Plant the bulb shoulders-up and water sparingly until the scape appears. Keep warm and well lit, rotating the pot for a straight stem. Flowers streaked and flecked in raspberry over white open around six to ten weeks, with each bloom marked a little differently.

planting.note: Spidery cybister form; force indoors for narrow, exotic petals.

directions: Set the bulb high in a snug pot and water lightly until growth begins. Give it bright warmth and turn the pot for balance. Slender, spidery petals in burgundy over green open in about six to ten weeks.

planting.note: Green-and-red cybister; grow indoors for unusual narrow blooms.

directions: Pot the bulb with its neck exposed and keep barely moist until the stem emerges. Provide strong light and rotate for even growth. Spidery petals in green and wine-red open roughly six to ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: Cybister type with a striking green throat; grow indoors.

directions: Plant the bulb high and water modestly until the flower stalk rises. Keep in bright warmth and turn the pot for a straight scape. Narrow petals in pale green with fine red picotee edging open in about six to ten weeks.

planting.note: Slim green spidery petals; a distinctive indoor cybister.

directions: Set the bulb with shoulders clear of the soil and water lightly at first. Grow in bright, warm conditions and feed lightly once leaves appear. Long, thin lime-green petals open roughly six to ten weeks after potting.

planting.note: Deep red cybister; grow indoors for narrow, curling petals.

directions: Pot the bulb high and keep the mix barely moist until growth starts. Provide strong light and rotate the pot for straight stems. Slim, recurving petals in dark ruby-red open in about six to ten weeks.

planting.note: Grow indoors for salmon-and-white two-toned blooms.

directions: Firm the bulb into a pot with its neck exposed and water sparingly until the bud shows. Keep in warm, bright light and feed lightly through growth. Coral-salmon petals with pale centers open roughly six to ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: Double picotee type; grow indoors with light staking.

directions: Plant the bulb high and add a stake to carry the full double blooms. Water carefully until active growth, then keep evenly moist and feed regularly. Ruffled white flowers edged in rose-pink open in about seven to eleven weeks.

planting.note: Double flowered; support the heavy stems indoors.

directions: Set the bulb with shoulders above the soil and stake early. Water lightly until buds form, then keep the mix evenly moist and feed lightly. Full double blooms in pink and white with green throats open in about seven to eleven weeks.

planting.note: Creamy-white single; force indoors for clean, bright flowers.

directions: Pot the bulb high and keep barely moist until the scape rises. Grow in bright warmth and turn for even stems. Large ivory-white trumpets with faint green throats open roughly six to ten weeks after potting.

planting.note: Large white heirloom; grow indoors in bright light.

directions: Plant the bulb with its neck exposed in a stable pot. Water modestly until growth begins, then increase and feed every couple of weeks. Very large pure white flowers open in about six to ten weeks.

planting.note: Vivid orange single; force indoors for warm color.

directions: Nest the bulb high in the pot and water lightly until the bud appears. Keep in strong light and steady warmth, rotating for straight stems. Bright orange trumpets open roughly six to ten weeks after planting.

planting.note: Soft pink-and-white striping; grow indoors.

directions: Pot the bulb shoulders-up and keep barely moist until the scape emerges. Provide bright warmth and turn the pot as it grows. Pale flowers finely veined in rose open in about six to ten weeks.

planting.note: Red with a white throat; force indoors for winter bloom.

directions: Set the bulb high in the pot and water sparingly until growth starts. Keep in bright, warm conditions and feed lightly once leaves develop. Red flowers with pale green-white centers open roughly six to ten weeks after potting.

planting.note: Tropical American species; grow indoors or in frost-free gardens.

directions: Plant the bulb with shoulders above the soil and water moderately once growth begins. Provide warmth and bright, filtered light. Red flowers with a green-white star at the center open in roughly six to twelve weeks.

planting.note: Easy Brazilian species that offsets freely; grow in pots or mild gardens.

directions: Set the bulb high in a well-drained pot and water moderately during growth. It clumps readily, so divide offsets every few years to keep it blooming well. Orange-red petals with a greenish base and fine central stripe open in about six to twelve weeks.

planting.note: Widely naturalized tropical species; grows outdoors in warm regions.

directions: Plant the bulb with its neck exposed in free-draining soil and water moderately in the growing season. In frost-free gardens it can be left in the ground to naturalize. Orange to salmon-red flowers with a green-yellow throat open in about six to twelve weeks.

planting.note: Autumn-to-winter blooming species from Brazil; grow indoors in cool climates.

directions: Pot the bulb high and keep lightly moist, as this species often flowers in late autumn or early winter. Give bright, filtered light and cooler nights while in bud. Crimson petals with a green throat open several weeks after growth resumes.

planting.note: Autumn-blooming species; the striped-leaf form has a white midrib.

directions: Plant the bulb in a well-drained pot and keep evenly moist, since this species stays semi-evergreen. Grow in bright, filtered light and protect from cold. Soft mauve-pink flowers with fine darker veining open in autumn, several weeks after new growth.

planting.note: Plant the large bulb in spring in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set the massive bulb with its long neck above the soil and give it plenty of room, as it resents disturbance once established. Water generously in the growing season and mulch in colder zones. Clusters of fragrant pink or white trumpets appear in late summer.

planting.note: Grow as a shade houseplant; needs a cool winter rest to bloom well.

directions: Pot the fleshy-rooted plant in well-drained mix and keep it slightly rootbound in bright, indirect light. Give it a cool, nearly dry spell for six to eight weeks in winter to trigger flowering. Rounded clusters of orange, trumpet-shaped blooms open in late winter or spring.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring once frost danger has passed.

directions: Set bulbs two to three inches deep in well-drained soil and water freely during warm weather. Large rose-pink funnel-shaped blooms appear within days of a soaking rain, often repeating from summer into autumn. Lift and store bulbs over winter where the ground freezes hard.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in frost-free or container culture.

directions: Plant the small bulbs an inch or two deep in a fast-draining mix, several to a pot for the best show. Deep rose-pink starry flowers appear in flushes through the warm months following rain or watering. Bring containers indoors before frost in cold regions.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring; they tolerate heat and drought once set.

directions: Plant these large bulbs three to four inches deep in gritty, well-drained ground. Big white long-tubed flowers open in the evening after summer storms and release a soft fragrance overnight. Very drought-tolerant, this Texas native asks for little once established.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in lean, well-drained soil.

directions: Bury the bulbs about three inches deep where they will bake in summer sun. Slender-tubed white flowers unfurl in the evening after rain and fade to pinkish the next day. It thrives on neglect in dry, sunny borders and rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after the last frost.

directions: Set bulbs two inches deep in rich, moisture-retentive soil. Soft primrose-yellow flowers open flat and fade toward apricot as they age, appearing repeatedly after warm rains. Keep the clumps watered during dry spells to extend the bloom season.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in a warm, sheltered spot.

directions: Plant bulbs about two inches deep in a container or a warm, well-drained bed. Clear yellow flowers with a greenish throat rise on short stems after summer moisture. Grow it in pots and shelter from frost in all but the mildest gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring; tolerates damp ground.

directions: Tuck the small bulbs an inch or two deep, ideally where the soil stays reliably moist. This South American species carries bright yellow flowers above narrow, wiry leaves through the warm season. It multiplies steadily into a cheerful colony over a few years.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in a sunny, warm bed.

directions: Plant bulbs two inches deep in a warm border with good drainage. Rich rose-pink flowers appear in flushes after summer rains and stand well above the foliage. Divide crowded clumps every few years to keep flowering strong.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in lean, gritty soil.

directions: Set the small bulbs about two inches deep in sharply drained ground. This compact Texas native opens crisp white flowers after warm-season rain. It shrugs off heat and drought, making it a fine choice for rock gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Plant bulbs two to three inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Bright pink flowers with an unusually long floral tube emerge after summer rains. Water generously during active growth for the fullest display of blooms.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in fall for late-winter to spring bloom.

directions: Plant bulbs two to three inches deep in moist, sandy soil at a woodland edge. This Florida native lifts white flowers, often pink-tinged on the outside, in late winter and spring. Give it seasonally damp ground that dries somewhat in summer dormancy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: Set bulbs two inches deep in gritty, quick-draining soil. Warm copper-yellow flowers, sometimes flushed pink, open after summer rains. This heat-loving rain lily suits sunny borders in the lower South.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in well-drained ground.

directions: Plant the small bulbs two inches deep in lean, well-drained soil. Pale creamy-yellow flowers rise after warm rains above fine, grassy foliage. It is a tough, drought-adapted native for hot, sunny spots.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in dry, sunny ground.

directions: Plant bulbs two to three inches deep in sharply drained, sandy soil. Small copper-yellow flowers, bronzed on the outside, open after monsoon rains in its southwestern range. Extremely drought-tolerant, it needs no summer irrigation once established.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost danger passes.

directions: Set bulbs two to three inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil. Large deep-pink to rosy-red flowers appear generously after summer rains. This vigorous Mexican species is among the showier pink rain lilies for warm gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in a warm, sunny bed.

directions: Plant bulbs about two inches deep in well-drained soil. White flowers washed with pink open after warm rains above slim foliage. A delicate, free-flowering Mexican species best grown where winters stay mild.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Plant the bulbs two inches deep and grow in full sun for the best flowering. This long-cultivated hybrid opens soft primrose-yellow flowers reliably through the warm season. Increase your stock by lifting and dividing the fast-multiplying offsets.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set bulbs two to three inches deep in rich, well-drained ground. This robust hybrid carries large pale-yellow flowers freely after summer rains. Its bigger stature and generous bloom make it a standout in the front of a border.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in a warm, sunny site.

directions: Plant the large bulbs three inches deep in well-drained, fertile soil. This selection is prized for big, showy rose-pink flowers that repeat after warm rains. Water freely in summer and it will bloom in successive waves.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs two to three inches deep in a sunny bed with good drainage. This large hybrid opens warm yellow flowers flushed with peach and pink tones. Deadhead spent blooms and keep watered to encourage repeat flushes through late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in a sunny, well-drained spot.

directions: Set bulbs two to three inches deep in fertile, quick-draining soil. This popular hybrid produces deep rose-pink flowers freely after warm rains. Divide the clumps every few years to keep the flowering vigorous.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after the soil warms.

directions: Plant bulbs two to three inches deep in well-drained soil, several to a group for impact. This close rain-lily relative lifts large, upward-facing pink flowers a day or two after a soaking rain. It seeds around gently and multiplies into a pretty colony.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in lean, well-drained ground.

directions: Set the small bulbs two inches deep in gritty, well-drained soil. Coppery-orange flowers with bronze reverses open after summer and fall rains. This tough, seed-happy species naturalizes readily in warm, sunny gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in well-drained soil.

directions: Plant bulbs two to three inches deep in a sunny, well-drained bed. Soft pink flowers with a paler throat rise on slender stems after warm rains. It forms tidy clumps and rewards summer watering with repeat bloom.

planting.note: Divide and replant offsets in spring as growth resumes.

directions: Position the bulb neck at the soil line in fertile, moisture-retentive ground and keep it evenly watered. This pure-white form is best increased by lifting offsets, since it will not come true from seed. Tall scapes carry lily-like fragrant blooms from mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant along pond margins or in consistently moist beds in spring.

directions: This native wetland bulb thrives in saturated soil or shallow standing water; set the neck at the surface. Keep it wet year-round and give it sun to light shade. Fragrant white spider-like flowers with pink-tinged stamens appear from spring into fall.

planting.note: Plant after frost in rich, moist soil with some shade.

directions: Plant the rounded bulb shallowly in fertile, well-drained soil that stays evenly moist. Broad, ribbed leaves form a lush rosette; give afternoon shade in hot climates. Pure-white bell-shaped flowers open on short scapes mainly in summer and fall.

planting.note: Plant after frost in warm, moist, fertile ground.

directions: Set the large bulb with its neck exposed in rich soil and water well through the warm season. It needs frost-free conditions or lifting for winter in cold areas. Striped white-and-wine trumpets carry a strong evening fragrance in summer.

planting.note: Plant or divide bulbs in spring in rich, moist soil.

directions: Plant this old garden hybrid with the neck above soil and keep it well fed and watered. Long-lived clumps are best increased by dividing offsets. White trumpets boldly striped in raspberry appear on tall stalks in summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost in warm, fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant the bulb shallowly in rich soil in a warm, sunny position and water freely in summer. Provide frost protection or grow in containers in cold regions. Pink-flushed, white-striped trumpets open on tall scapes through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant after frost near water or in reliably moist soil.

directions: This robust Australian species thrives in wet soil and by pond edges; set the neck at the surface. Give it full sun and steady water for the biggest clumps of arching leaves. Fragrant white spidery flowers rise on thick stalks through summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost in a warm, sunny, well-drained bed.

directions: This drought-tolerant Australian bulb suits hot, well-drained sites; plant the neck at soil level. Water during growth but let it dry between times, mimicking its seasonal habitat. Fragrant creamy-white flowers appear in summer, sometimes after rain.

planting.note: Plant after frost in deep, fertile soil in full sun.

directions: Set the very large bulb deeply in rich soil, as this African species roots to considerable depth. Water well in the growing season and allow a drier rest in winter. Pink-striped white trumpets open in a rounded head on a stout scape in summer.

planting.note: Plant in spring in a seasonally moist, sunny site.

directions: Plant this South African bulb in soil that is wet in the growing season and drier at rest. Give it full sun and protect from hard freezes. Nodding pink-and-white flowers open in a loose head in late summer and autumn.

planting.note: Plant in spring where it can sit in water during the growing season.

directions: A marsh species happiest with its roots submerged in summer; plant in a pot stood in water or a boggy bed. Let it dry somewhat after flowering during its winter rest. Bell-shaped rose-red flowers with pale centers appear in summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost and give this huge, wine-leaved form generous space.

directions: Plant the enormous bulb in warm, rich soil where its burgundy-flushed leaves can spread wide. It is strictly frost-tender and best increased from offsets. White-to-pink fragrant flowers open above the dramatic foliage in the warm season; all parts are toxic.

planting.note: Plant or divide bulbs in spring in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Set the bulb with its neck above soil in fertile ground and water well through summer. This tough, popular hybrid forms long-lived clumps best increased by division. Deep rose-wine trumpets with a light fragrance appear repeatedly from early to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring; a reliable, free-flowering old hybrid.

directions: Plant the bulb neck at the surface in good soil and keep it watered in the growing season. Among the most dependable bloomers, it multiplies freely into large clumps. Clear pink trumpets open over a long stretch from late spring into fall.

planting.note: Plant or divide in spring in rich, moist garden soil.

directions: Plant this classic passalong bulb with its neck exposed and feed it for strong growth. Established clumps are long-lived and easily divided. White trumpets streaked with wine-red open on tall scapes in summer, carrying a sweet scent.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set this compact old Southern hybrid with the neck at soil level and water regularly in summer. It stays smaller than many crinums, suiting borders. Blush-pink, sweetly fragrant flowers fading toward white open in summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in deep, rich soil for the boldest color.

directions: Plant the bulb neck above soil and give it full sun to bring out the deep foliage and flower tones. Water well in summer and mulch where winters are cold. Rich wine-red trumpets rise on dark-tinged scapes through summer.

planting.note: Plant after frost; a compact selection good for containers.

directions: This dwarf, burgundy-leaved crinum suits pots and small beds; plant the bulb shallowly in rich mix. Keep it evenly moist and shelter it from frost. Rose-pink flowers appear above the dark compact foliage in the warm season.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant the bulb neck at the surface and water freely through the growing season. It forms a strong clump increased by division. Broad, open white flowers with a faint blush open on tall scapes in summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in deep, rich soil.

directions: Set the bulb with its neck above soil and keep it watered in summer for best growth. This vigorous hybrid multiplies into large clumps over time. Deep rosy-red trumpets appear on tall stalks through the summer months.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Plant the bulb neck at the surface in full sun and water through the warm season. Divide crowded clumps in spring to increase your stock. Boldly bicolored striped trumpets open on tall scapes in summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant the bulb with its neck above the soil and keep it well watered in summer. It builds into a substantial clump over several seasons. Deep pink trumpets rise on tall stalks through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile garden soil.

directions: Set the bulb neck at the surface and water regularly through the growing season. This graceful hybrid forms long-lasting clumps increased by offsets. Soft double-form pink-and-white flowers open on arching scapes in summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in deep, rich soil.

directions: Plant the bulb neck above soil and water well in summer for vigorous foliage. A tough passalong hybrid that multiplies readily into big clumps. Silvery-pink trumpets with a darker keel open on tall scapes in summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant the bulb neck at the surface and keep it watered through the season. Divide established clumps in spring to propagate. Soft peachy-pink flowers open over a long summer season.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in rich garden soil.

directions: Plant the bulb with its neck above soil and water freely in summer. A robust hybrid that forms substantial, free-flowering clumps. Pink trumpets with a paler throat open on tall scapes through summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in deep, fertile soil.

directions: Plant the bulb neck at the surface in full sun and water through the growing season. This larger, more vigorous descendant of Ellen Bosanquet builds tall clumps. Deep rose-wine trumpets open on stout scapes through summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the bulb neck at the surface and water regularly in summer. Divide crowded clumps in spring to increase. White trumpets with a bold pink stripe down each petal open on tall scapes in summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in rich, moisture-retentive soil.

directions: Plant the bulb neck above soil and water well through the season. A vigorous, showy hybrid that forms large clumps over time. Bright pink striped trumpets open in generous heads through summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile garden soil.

directions: Plant the bulb neck at the surface and keep it watered in summer. Prized for evening fragrance, it forms a long-lived clump. Fragrant pink-blushed white trumpets open on tall scapes through the summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in deep, rich soil for darkest color.

directions: Plant the bulb neck above soil in full sun to develop its dark foliage tones. Water well in summer and divide crowded clumps in spring. Deep maroon-red trumpets open on dark scapes through the warm months.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile, well-drained soil.

directions: Set the bulb neck at the surface and water regularly through summer. A hardy Texas passalong that forms strong, reliable clumps. White trumpets striped in wine-red open on tall scapes in summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in rich, well-drained soil; this bigeneric hybrid is sterile.

directions: Plant the bulb with its neck at soil level in a warm, sunny, well-drained spot and leave it undisturbed to bloom well. This cross of Amaryllis and Crinum is sterile and increased only by offsets. Clusters of fragrant clear-pink trumpets rise on sturdy stalks from late summer into fall.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in fertile, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Set the bulb neck at soil level and give it a warm, sunny position with good drainage. Leave clumps in place for years and increase by dividing offsets. Fragrant rich-pink flowers open in a rounded head on strong stems from late summer into autumn.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring in rich, well-drained soil in full sun.

directions: Plant the bulb neck at soil level in a sunny, freely draining bed and allow it to settle in undisturbed. It is sterile and multiplied by offsets. Fragrant deep-pink trumpets cluster atop upright stems from late summer through fall.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after the last frost when soil is warming.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly 4 to 5 inches deep in fertile soil that drains freely. The green flowers are edged in purple and carried in a dense spike crowned with a leafy top. Look for bloom in late summer, and reduce watering as the foliage yellows in autumn.

planting.note: Plant the large bulbs in spring after frost in deep, rich soil.

directions: Plant these large bulbs about 6 inches deep with plenty of room, as the flower stalks can top five feet. Provide steady moisture and fertile ground to support the tall spike, and stake in windy sites. The towering pale green flower column opens in late summer and is a dramatic focal point.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring; ideal for containers and rock gardens.

directions: Plant the small bulbs about 3 inches deep in gritty, sharply drained soil or a container. The wavy leaves are mottled maroon and the compact wine-red flower spikes sit low over the foliage. Keep on the dry side when dormant and expect bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost in a warm, bright spot.

directions: Set bulbs about 4 inches deep in well-drained soil or a pot of gritty mix. This compact species produces clean white flower spikes on short stalks. Water freely in summer, then keep dry through winter dormancy, and enjoy bloom in mid summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring once soil warms and frost has passed.

directions: Plant bulbs about 5 inches deep in rich, free-draining soil in full sun. New leaves emerge deep burgundy-purple and hold color best in strong light before greening slightly by summer. The dark stems carry pale pink flower spikes in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after the last frost.

directions: Set bulbs roughly 5 inches deep in fertile, well-drained soil in a sunny bed. The foliage flushes rich burgundy and the sturdy dark stems bear dusky pink spikes. Feed and water through summer, then hold dry over winter where it is left in the ground under mulch.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring once frost has passed.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 to 5 inches deep in a warm, sunny position with sharp drainage. This compact selection carries deep rosy-pink flower spikes on dark stems. Keep evenly moist during growth and expect bloom through mid and late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring; excellent in patio containers.

directions: Plant bulbs about 4 inches deep in a container of free-draining potting mix or a warm border. This dwarf selection stays compact and blooms freely with pale pink spikes. Water regularly in summer, then keep nearly dry during dormancy.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after frost in full sun.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep in rich soil that drains well. The plant carries wine-red toned flower spikes above bold strappy leaves. Provide steady summer moisture, then let it rest dry as leaves die back in autumn.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring once soil warms.

directions: Plant bulbs roughly 5 inches deep in fertile, well-drained ground in full sun. This selection produces cool jade-green flower spikes topped with the signature leafy tuft. Water freely through the growing season and expect bloom in mid to late summer.

planting.note: Plant bulbs in spring after the last frost.

directions: Set bulbs about 5 inches deep in a sunny, free-draining bed. The leaf backs and stems are spotted and speckled with purple freckling, giving the plant its name. Creamy flower spikes rise in mid to late summer above the marked foliage.
